back: A linear transformation T: V→W is a mapping between vector spaces that preserves vector addition and scalar multiplication: T(u+v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v) for all vectors u,v in V and scalars c.

front: What is the Superposition Property of Linear Transformations?
back: The superposition property combines additivity T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v) and homogeneity T(cv)=cT(v). This means T(c₁v₁ + c₂v₂) = c₁T(v₁) + c₂T(v₂).

front: Define Kernel (Null Space) of a Linear Transformation
back: The kernel (or null space) of T: V→W is the set of all vectors in V that map to the zero vector in W: Ker(T) = {v ∈ V | T(v) = 0}.

front: Define Range (Image) of a Linear Transformation
back: The range (or image) of T: V→W is the set of all vectors in W that are images of vectors in V: Range(T) = {T(v) | v ∈ V}.

front: What is a One-to-One (Injective) Linear Transformation?
back: A linear transformation T: V→W is one-to-one if different inputs produce different outputs: T(u) = T(v) implies u = v. Equivalently, Ker(T) = {0}.

front: What is an Onto (Surjective) Linear Transformation?
back: A linear transformation T: V→W is onto if every vector in W is the image of some vector in V: Range(T) = W.
An isomorphism is a linear transformation that is both one-to-one and onto. Two vector spaces are isomorphic if there exists an isomorphism between them.

front: Define the Standard Matrix of a Linear Transformation
back: For T: ℝⁿ→ℝᵐ, the standard matrix A is an m×n matrix where the jth column is T(ej) for standard basis ej. T(x) = Ax.
For T: V→W with V finite-dimensional: dim(Ker(T)) + dim(Range(T)) = dim(V). Nullity + Rank = Dimension of domain.
If S: U→V and T: V→W are linear transformations, their composition T∘S: U→W is defined by (T∘S)(u) = T(S(u)). The matrix representation is the product of matrices.

front: What is an Inverse Linear Transformation?
back: A linear transformation T: V→W has an inverse T⁻¹: W→V if T is one-to-one and onto. If A is the matrix of T, then T⁻¹ exists iff A is invertible.

front: How do you verify if a transformation is Linear?
back: Check two properties: (1) T(u+v) = T(u) + T(v) for all u,v; (2) T(cv) = cT(v) for all vectors v and scalars c. Equivalently, T(c₁v₁ + c₂v₂) = c₁T(v₁) + c₂T(v₂).
